nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving blackwater peat swamps, marshes, rice paddies, and vegetated floodplain pools; tannin-stained water with dense fine-leaved aquatic macrophytes and minimal current

decorPreferences: dense fine-leaved planting (Java moss, hornwort, Riccia) for cover and egg scattering
floating plants to diffuse light and reduce skittishness
driftwood and botanicals (Indian almond leaves, alder cones) to acidify and tannin-stain water
dark substrate to reduce stress and enhance coloration

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on tiny insects, worms, zooplankton, and small crustaceans. In captivity accepts micro-pellets, finely crushed flake, and powder foods. Live and frozen micro-foods — baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, micro worms — are strongly recommended for optimal coloration and conditioning for breeding. Mouth size limits food to very small items; standard-sized daphnia or brine shrimp may be too large.

goodWith: other Boraras species (B. brigittae, B. merah)
dwarf shrimp (cherry, crystal — adults safe; shrimp may eat eggs)
avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them (virtually all standard community fish)
fast or boisterous species that cause chronic stress

imageCandidates.notes: Wild-caught specimen in aquarium, non-breeding coloration. Photographer: Melanochromis (2008). 540×360 px. Fish was recently caught so color is subdued.

summary: The Exclamation Point Rasbora is one of the world's smallest aquarium fish (~2 cm), named for the distinctive dash-and-dot mark on its flank. A blackwater specialist from SE Asia, it thrives in soft, acidic, tannin-stained nano setups and is safe with dwarf shrimp. Keep in groups of 10+.
careNotes:
Boraras urophthalmoides is among the smallest aquarium fish available, reaching only about 2 cm in length. The genus Boraras was formerly classified within Rasbora and was split into its own genus in 1993; the name is an anagram of Rasbora. The species is sometimes confused with the Chili Rasbora (B. brigittae), which has a vivid continuous scarlet lateral stripe, and the Phoenix Rasbora (B. merah), which shows a single mid-body blotch — the Exclamation Point Rasbora is distinguished by its unique dash-plus-caudal-spot pattern (the 'exclamation mark') and a generally more translucent, pale coloration compared to the deeper reds of its congeners.

This species demands soft, acidic water reflecting its blackwater peat-swamp origins. A pH of 5.0–6.5 with very low hardness (gH 1–8, kH 0–4) is ideal; some keepers maintain it in slightly higher pH (to 7.0) with good results, but alkaline or hard water shortens lifespan and suppresses breeding. Tannin-stained water from Indian almond leaves, alder cones, or driftwood is strongly recommended and visibly intensifies coloration. A sponge filter or other very low-flow filtration is essential — the species evolved in near-stagnant waters and is easily stressed by current. Tank lighting should be subdued; floating plants help.

Groups of at least 10 individuals are needed to bring out natural shoaling behavior and reduce chronic shyness. Fewer specimens results in nervous, hiding fish that rarely display. At adult size (~2 cm), they pose no predation risk to adult dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina), making them one of the few fish genuinely compatible with shrimp colonies — though shrimp may consume eggs. Virtually all standard community fish are too large or too boisterous to be suitable tankmates.
breedingNotes: Continuous egg-scatterers with no parental care; well-conditioned groups in planted tanks will spawn daily. Both sexes eat their own eggs readily — a breeding setup should include a mesh bottom or thick Java moss/Riccia carpet that lets eggs fall out of reach. Females are noticeably rounder when gravid; males develop brighter coloration during courtship. Eggs hatch in approximately 48 hours at 26–28°C. Fry are nearly transparent ('slivers of glass') and require infusoria or green water as first foods, transitioning to micro worms and baby brine shrimp nauplii within a week. Spawning is most reliable in soft acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–6) at 26–28°C with frequent small live-food feedings to condition adults.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Boraras urophthalmoides
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (12–16 mm SL), temperature 20–28°C, pH 6.0–7.0, hardness 18–179 ppm, native range (Thailand, Vietnam, Cambodia), habitat (swamps/marshes/rice paddies), diet (micropredator: Daphnia, Artemia, small insects), minimum tank base 45×30 cm (~41 L), grouping (8–10 minimum), and breeding (continuous egg-scatterer, ~2-day hatch).

additional.name: FishBase — Boraras urophthalmoides
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (4.0 cm TL reported — likely includes outliers or measurement artefacts; 2 cm used per SeriouslyFish SL and multi-source consensus), taxonomy (Danionidae/Rasborinae under Cypriniformes — Cyprinidae retained per user brief as hobby-standard family name), habitat (peat/bog wetlands, marshes, swamps with dense macrophytes), IUCN Near Threatened status, and distribution (Asia; Vietnam trade use noted).

additional.name: Gensou Premium Aquascaping — Exclamation Point Rasbora Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size ~2 cm, temperature 22–28°C, pH 5.0–7.0, gH 1–8 dGH, kH 0–4 dKH, native range Thailand/Cambodia/Vietnam, habitat (shallow slow-moving water, rice paddies, peat swamp margins), minimum 10 fish for natural behavior (15–20 preferred), minimum 20–40 L tank, compatibility with cherry/crystal shrimp and nano congeners, and tannin-stained water recommendation for color enhancement.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Exclamation Point Rasbora Care
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size ~2.5 cm (1 in), temperature 20–27°C, pH 6.0–7.0, gH 8–12, lifespan 4–8 years, diet (omnivore tendency; live/frozen micro-foods best for coloration), minimum 15 gal for school of 8–12, compatible tankmates (pygmy corydoras, ember tetras, cherry shrimp), and distinguishing features vs chili rasbora (translucent fins vs vivid scarlet in B. brigittae).

additional.name: TFH Magazine — The Genus Boraras: Miniature Jewels
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 16 mm, common names (exclamation point / sparrow rasbora), blackwater habitat preference, successful breeding in soft acidic conditions, fry feeding on biofilm around driftwood, strong sexual dimorphism (males bright red), and genus note that Boraras was split from Rasbora (formerly Rasbora urophthalmoides).

additional.name: PVAS — Boraras urophthalmoides the Exclamation Point Rasbora
additional.notes: Hobbyist breeding account. Confirmed very soft, acidic water (pH 5.5, 16 ppm TDS) for breeding, spawning trigger at 78°F (26°C), fry description (transparent, very small), rearing on green water and rotifers, and natural habitat near Sai Buri River in southern Thailand.

fish.breedingNotes: Continuous egg-scatterer; spawns daily in conditioned groups but eggs are eaten by parents without protection measures. Requires soft acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5), 26–28°C, and live food conditioning. Fry are extremely small and need infusoria/green water as first foods.
